subsystem: TensorFlow Lite builtin operator kernel: the Eval (invoke) entry point for the EMBEDDING_LOOKUP_SPARSE op. It takes a sparse tensor described by (ids, indices, dense_shape), an embedding table (value), and per-lookup weights, resizes the output tensor, and accumulates weighted embedding rows into aggregation buckets according to a combiner stored in the op's builtin params.
provenance: Identified from TFLite kernel idioms and symbols: the signature TfLiteStatus Eval(TfLiteContext*, TfLiteNode*), TF_LITE_ENSURE_OK / TF_LITE_ENSURE / TF_LITE_ENSURE_EQ / TF_LITE_ENSURE_MSG / TF_LITE_ENSURE_STATUS macros, GetInputSafe/GetOutputSafe, SizeOfDimension/NumDimensions/NumElements, TfLiteIntArrayCreate, GetTensorData<float>, TfLiteTensorRealloc, and the params type reinterpret_cast<TfLiteEmbeddingLookupSparseParams*>(node->builtin_data). The header banner names corpus/train/train_0132_cwe190.cpp and the in-source tag `/* sample: primevul_train_2060_fixed */` marks this as the post-fix revision of a PrimeVul CWE-190 sample.
trust_boundary: The tensors ids, indices, dense_shape, weights, and value are model-supplied inputs. In TFLite, an attacker who supplies a .tflite model or feeds a malicious serialized graph controls every dimension (SizeOfDimension/NumDimensions/NumElements) and every element of these int32/float buffers (ids->data.i32, indices->data.i32, dense_shape->data.i32, weights_ptr, value_ptr). node->builtin_data is also model-derived. Hostile fields therefore include: lookup_rank, embedding_rank, num_lookups, num_rows, the dense_shape entries used as multiplicands, the ids indices, and the indices coordinates used to compute bucket offsets. Nothing here is a network wire format, but a crafted model is untrusted input and this Eval runs it in-process.

what_the_code_does.explanation: This is the runtime evaluation callback the TFLite interpreter invokes for each EMBEDDING_LOOKUP_SPARSE node. context carries the error-reporting and tensor-management API; node holds the op's input/output tensor indices and its builtin_data blob. The first statement reinterpret_casts the opaque node->builtin_data pointer to a TfLiteEmbeddingLookupSparseParams*, from which the code later reads params->combiner (line 94, 123) to choose how to aggregate weighted embeddings. reinterpret_cast performs no runtime type or layout check; it trusts that the flatbuffer loader populated builtin_data with exactly this struct type.

The kernel fetches its one output tensor and five input tensors by fixed operand index. GetOutputSafe/GetInputSafe are the hardened accessors that validate the operand index and non-null tensor pointer, returning a non-OK status that TF_LITE_ENSURE_OK propagates as an early return. The sparse tensor is encoded across three inputs: ids (input 0, the row lookup values), indices (input 1, the sparse coordinate matrix), and dense_shape (input 2, the logical shape). weights (input 3) supplies a scalar weight per lookup, and value (input 4) is the dense embedding table whose rows are gathered. Note these accessors validate the tensor handle exists, not that the tensors' shapes are mutually consistent.

values_size is the total element count of the embedding table, later used as the upper bound for value_ptr reads (line 116). lookup_rank is dimension 1 of the indices matrix (the number of sparse coordinate columns). embedding_rank is the number of dimensions of the embedding table. num_lookups is dimension 0 of ids (how many lookups to perform, i.e. how many rows of indices/weights to process). num_rows is dimension 0 of value, the number of embedding rows against which each id is range-checked. All four are read directly from attacker-controlled tensor shapes and are plain signed ints, so any of them could be zero or, depending on how the shapes were built, drive degenerate loop bounds.

output_rank is the rank of the result: the sparse tensor contributes (lookup_rank - 1) leading dimensions and the embedding contributes (embedding_rank - 1) trailing dimensions (the innermost lookup axis is replaced by the embedding). The single consistency gate here is TF_LITE_ENSURE_EQ that dense_shape's length equals lookup_rank, which guarantees dense_shape has lookup_rank entries so that the later reads dense_shape->data.i32[i] (lines 38, 87) stay in bounds. Crucially, this is the ONLY cross-tensor shape check in the whole kernel: there is no check that indices has num_lookups rows, nor that weights has num_lookups elements.

output_shape is allocated to hold output_rank dimension entries; the code checks the allocation succeeded (guarding against a NULL from TfLiteIntArrayCreate). k is a shared write cursor into output_shape->data used by both dimension-filling loops. embedding_size and lookup_size accumulate, by multiplication, the number of elements contributed by the embedding sub-shape and the lookup sub-shape respectively; both are size_t and initialized to 1 so the running products are well-defined.

This loop fills the leading (lookup_rank - 1) output dimensions from dense_shape entries and simultaneously accumulates lookup_size = product of those dims. dim is read from the int32 dense_shape buffer and widened to size_t. The accumulation goes through MultiplyAndCheckOverflow, which returns non-kTfLiteOk if lookup_size * dim would overflow size_t, and TF_LITE_ENSURE_MSG turns that into an error return. This overflow guard is the defensive addition that distinguishes this 'fixed' revision. Note that a negative dense_shape entry becomes an enormous size_t after widening, which the overflow check will usually catch, but the raw negative value is also written into output_shape->data[k] as the resized dimension.

This loop fills the trailing output dimensions from the embedding table's shape (dimensions 1..embedding_rank-1, i.e. every embedding dimension except the row axis) and accumulates embedding_size = product of those dims, again through MultiplyAndCheckOverflow. embedding_size is the per-row embedding element count and is later used as the stride when scattering values into output buckets (lines 89, 107, 111). k continues from where the previous loop stopped so the two sub-shapes are laid out contiguously in output_shape.

ResizeTensor commits output_shape as the output tensor's dimensions. output_size is then computed as lookup_size * embedding_size, the total float count of the output, and TfLiteTensorRealloc reallocates the output buffer to output_size * sizeof(float) bytes. This is the pivotal arithmetic: unlike the two accumulation loops, this final product lookup_size * embedding_size is NOT passed through MultiplyAndCheckOverflow, and neither is the subsequent output_size * sizeof(float). Both are plain size_t multiplications of attacker-influenced magnitudes.

The code obtains raw float pointers to the output, weights, and embedding buffers. It checks output_ptr != nullptr to detect a failed reallocation (TfLiteTensorRealloc's success is inferred only from this null check, not from a returned status). std::fill_n then zeroes output_size floats through output_ptr; this both initializes the accumulator and, importantly, is bounded by the same output_size used for allocation, so it is self-consistent with the buffer size. weights_ptr and value_ptr are not null-checked here.

These are the running-aggregation accumulators. current_output_offset is the base element offset into output_ptr for the bucket currently being accumulated (an int). current_total_weight and current_squares_weight accumulate the weight sum and sum-of-squares used by mean/sqrtn combiners, and num_elements counts contributions in the current bucket. current_output_offset starts at 0, and is later reassigned from an attacker-derived output_offset (line 102).

The main loop iterates over the num_lookups lookups. For each, idx = ids->data.i32[i] reads the requested embedding row from the ids buffer, and the code range-checks idx against [0, num_rows-1], rejecting out-of-range rows with an error. This guard protects the embedding-row dimension of the value table. The loop reads ids->data.i32[i] for i < num_lookups where num_lookups is ids's own dimension 0, so the ids read itself is in bounds.

For lookup i, example_indices_offset = i * lookup_rank locates row i of the indices matrix. The inner loop folds the leading (lookup_rank-1) sparse coordinates into a linear bucket index output_bucket using a mixed-radix scheme, with stride multiplied by successive dense_shape dimensions. output_offset = output_bucket * embedding_size converts the bucket index into an element offset into the output buffer. Every quantity here — the indices coordinates, the dense_shape strides, and thus output_bucket and output_offset — is derived from attacker-controlled int32 tensor data, and all of this arithmetic is signed int (output_bucket, stride, output_offset), while embedding_size is size_t.

When the computed output_offset differs from the bucket currently being accumulated, the previous bucket is finalized by FinalizeAggregation, which is handed &output_ptr[current_output_offset] as the destination and embedding_size as the span to write, plus the combiner mode and accumulated weights. The accumulators are then reset and current_output_offset is advanced to the new output_offset. Critically, current_output_offset is used here as the base of a write of embedding_size floats WITHOUT any prior bounds check that current_output_offset (and current_output_offset + embedding_size) lies within output_size.

For the current lookup, example_embedding_offset = idx * embedding_size is the base offset of embedding row idx in the value table, and w = weights_ptr[i] is the lookup's weight (read from the weights buffer at index i). The inner loop scatters the weighted embedding row into the output bucket. This loop is the fixed revision's defensive core: each iteration explicitly bounds-checks current_output_offset + k against [0, output_size) before writing output_ptr, and example_embedding_offset + k against [0, values_size) before reading value_ptr, skipping any out-of-range element. Those four guards make the per-element read and write here memory-safe even for wild offsets. Note that weights_ptr[i] on line 108 is read with NO such guard against the weights buffer length.

After the loop, the final open bucket is finalized the same way, writing embedding_size floats starting at output[current_output_offset]. As with the in-loop FinalizeAggregation (line 96), current_output_offset here is the last attacker-derived offset and is passed as a write base without a bounds check. The kernel then returns kTfLiteOk.

what_could_go_wrong.code:
const size_t output_size = lookup_size * embedding_size;
  TfLiteTensorRealloc(output_size * sizeof(float), output);
what_could_go_wrong.explanation: lookup_size and embedding_size are each individually guarded against overflow while being accumulated (lines 41, 48), but their PRODUCT on line 54, and the further multiplication output_size * sizeof(float) on line 55, are plain unchecked size_t multiplications — exactly the CWE-190 pattern the surrounding MultiplyAndCheckOverflow calls were added to prevent. An attacker choosing dense_shape and embedding dimensions whose sub-products are each representable but whose product wraps size_t makes output_size a small wrapped value; TfLiteTensorRealloc then allocates a buffer far smaller than the ResizeTensor dimensions (line 53) logically imply. Downstream, std::fill_n and the inner scatter loop use this same wrapped output_size as their bound, so the immediate scatter writes stay inside the (tiny) allocation, but the output tensor is now internally inconsistent: its declared shape says N elements while its buffer holds far fewer. Any consumer that trusts the tensor's declared shape/NumElements will read or write past the undersized buffer. The invariant is that lookup_size * embedding_size (and that times sizeof(float)) must be checked with MultiplyAndCheckOverflow before being used as an allocation size.

FinalizeAggregation receives &output_ptr[current_output_offset] as a destination and embedding_size as the count of floats to finalize/write there. current_output_offset was assigned from output_offset = output_bucket * embedding_size (lines 89, 102), where output_bucket is built entirely from attacker-controlled indices and dense_shape values with no clamp. Unlike the inner scatter loop (lines 113-116), this call site performs NO check that current_output_offset >= 0 and current_output_offset + embedding_size <= output_size before FinalizeAggregation dereferences the pointer. A crafted model that produces a large or negative bucket offset causes FinalizeAggregation to read/write embedding_size floats out of bounds of the output buffer — an out-of-bounds write (CWE-787) if it stores the aggregated result. The same unbounded use recurs at lines 123-125 for the final bucket. The invariant the implementation must uphold: current_output_offset must be validated within [0, output_size - embedding_size] before being used as a FinalizeAggregation base.

weights_ptr[i] is read for every i in [0, num_lookups), where num_lookups = SizeOfDimension(ids, 0) is the row count of the ids tensor. Nothing in this kernel (the only cross-tensor check is dense_shape length on line 29) verifies that the weights tensor actually contains num_lookups elements. If a crafted model supplies a weights tensor smaller than ids, weights_ptr[i] reads past the end of the weights buffer — an out-of-bounds read (CWE-125) of heap memory, which is then folded into current_total_weight/current_squares_weight and can influence output values (an information-influence / crash primitive). The invariant is that NumElements(weights) must be >= num_lookups (ideally validated in Prepare); it is not checked here.

indices->data.i32[example_indices_offset + k] indexes the indices buffer at i * lookup_rank + k for i in [0, num_lookups) and k in [0, lookup_rank-1). This assumes the indices matrix has num_lookups rows of lookup_rank columns, but the kernel never checks SizeOfDimension(indices, 0) == num_lookups — num_lookups comes from the ids tensor, while lookup_rank comes from indices dimension 1. If a crafted model gives indices fewer rows than ids has entries, example_indices_offset + k walks past the end of the indices buffer — an out-of-bounds read (CWE-125). The read values then feed output_bucket, so a wild read also steers the (unbounded) output offset. The invariant: indices must have exactly num_lookups rows; validate SizeOfDimension(indices, 0) against num_lookups before indexing.

output_bucket, stride, and output_offset are all signed int, accumulated from attacker-controlled indices coordinates and dense_shape strides. stride *= dense_shape->data.i32[k] and output_bucket += coord * stride can overflow a 32-bit signed int (undefined behavior, CWE-190), and output_offset = output_bucket * embedding_size mixes an int with a size_t and then truncates the size_t product back into an int, so a large or negative result is easy to produce. The downstream inner scatter loop re-validates current_output_offset + k against [0, output_size) so the per-element writes there remain safe, but this same tainted output_offset flows into current_output_offset and then into FinalizeAggregation (see the finding at lines 94-96), where it is NOT re-checked. The invariant: bucket/offset arithmetic should be done in a width that cannot overflow and the result must be range-checked before any use as a pointer base.

node->builtin_data is reinterpret_cast to TfLiteEmbeddingLookupSparseParams* with no verification of provenance, size, or that the flatbuffer actually carried this op's parameter type. If a malformed/mismatched model leaves builtin_data null or pointing at a differently-shaped blob, the later dereference params->combiner (lines 94, 123) is a type-confused / null-pointer read (CWE-704 / CWE-476). In practice the TFLite parser is expected to populate this correctly, so this is a lower-severity latent contract, but it is an unchecked trust in loader-supplied data.

summary: This is the post-fix EMBEDDING_LOOKUP_SPARSE TFLite kernel: it added MultiplyAndCheckOverflow guards on the per-axis size products and per-element bounds checks in the inner scatter loop, which close the original heap-overflow scatter. Residual latent risks remain: the final output_size = lookup_size * embedding_size (and output_size * sizeof(float)) multiplications are still unchecked CWE-190, and current_output_offset is passed to FinalizeAggregation as a write base without the bounds check the inner loop applies (potential CWE-787). Cross-tensor shape consistency (weights and indices row counts vs num_lookups) is also unvalidated, allowing out-of-bounds reads.

reviewed_anchors.reason: unchecked cast of loader-supplied opaque pointer; type-confusion / NULL-deref contract on params->combiner
hardened accessors validate operand index and non-null tensor; no length contract violated at the fetch itself
used correctly as the upper bound in the guarded value_ptr read (line 116)
attacker-controlled ranks/counts that drive loop bounds and cross-tensor indexing; feed the weights/indices over-read findings
only sizes the output_shape array which is allocated to exactly output_rank and null-checked; degenerate rank yields a small/empty array, not an overflow here
this is the guard that keeps dense_shape->data.i32[i]/[k] reads (lines 38,87) in bounds; correct as written
allocation is null-checked before use
correctly guards the per-axis lookup_size product against CWE-190
correctly guards the per-axis embedding_size product against CWE-190
unchecked size_t multiplication — the residual CWE-190 the file name flags
second unchecked multiplication feeding an allocation size; realloc status inferred only via later null check
correctly detects failed reallocation before use
bounded by the same output_size used to allocate; self-consistent
idx explicitly checked against [0, num_rows-1]; ids read is within ids dim0
indices row count never validated == num_lookups; potential CWE-125 over-read
signed int overflow / size_t-to-int truncation of attacker data feeding an offset used unchecked by FinalizeAggregation
current_output_offset used as write base with no bounds check unlike the inner loop; potential CWE-787
downstream reads guarded per-element against [0, values_size) (line 116); idx already range-checked
weights length never validated >= num_lookups; unguarded CWE-125 over-read
correctly bounds current_output_offset+k and example_embedding_offset+k before each read/write — the fix
same unchecked current_output_offset write base as lines 94-96 (covered by that finding)

findings.title: Unchecked output_size multiplication (residual integer overflow) drives undersized allocation
findings.anchor: const size_t output_size = lookup_size * embedding_size; TfLiteTensorRealloc(output_size * sizeof(float), output);
findings.code:
const size_t output_size = lookup_size * embedding_size;
  TfLiteTensorRealloc(output_size * sizeof(float), output);
findings.bug_class: Integer overflow in allocation size (unchecked size_t multiplication)
findings.invariant: lookup_size * embedding_size and (output_size * sizeof(float)) must be computed with MultiplyAndCheckOverflow (as the per-axis products already are) so that the reallocated buffer size matches the ResizeTensor'd shape and cannot wrap to a small value.
findings.failure_mode: An attacker picks embedding and lookup sub-shapes whose individual products pass MultiplyAndCheckOverflow (lines 41,48) but whose overall product lookup_size*embedding_size, or that value times sizeof(float), overflows size_t. output_size wraps to a small number, so TfLiteTensorRealloc allocates a buffer far smaller than the ResizeTensor'd declared shape (line 53). The output tensor is left with a declared element count that vastly exceeds its actual buffer, so any later op or consumer that trusts NumElements(output) reads/writes out of bounds.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm whether the product lookup_size*embedding_size and its *sizeof(float) are validated for overflow anywhere before TfLiteTensorRealloc, and whether ResizeTensor and the realloc size can diverge.

findings.title: current_output_offset used as FinalizeAggregation write base without bounds check
findings.bug_class: Out-of-bounds write (unvalidated offset used as pointer base)
findings.invariant: Before FinalizeAggregation dereferences &output_ptr[current_output_offset], the code must ensure current_output_offset >= 0 and current_output_offset + embedding_size <= output_size — the same bound the inner scatter loop applies per element.
findings.failure_mode: current_output_offset is set from output_offset = output_bucket * embedding_size (lines 89,102), where output_bucket is built entirely from attacker-controlled indices/dense_shape with no clamp and can be large or negative. FinalizeAggregation (lines 94-96 and again at 123-125) writes embedding_size floats starting at output_ptr[current_output_offset] with no bounds check, so a crafted model that produces an out-of-range bucket offset causes an out-of-bounds write (heap corruption) inside FinalizeAggregation even though the per-element scatter loop is guarded.
findings.what_to_confirm: Inspect FinalizeAggregation's body to confirm it writes to the passed pointer, and verify no caller-side clamp on current_output_offset exists before lines 96 and 125.

findings.title: weights buffer indexed by num_lookups without length validation (out-of-bounds read)
findings.invariant: NumElements(weights) must be >= num_lookups (= SizeOfDimension(ids,0)) before weights_ptr[i] is read for i in [0, num_lookups).
findings.failure_mode: num_lookups is derived from the ids tensor, but weights is a separate input whose element count is never checked. A model with a weights tensor shorter than ids makes weights_ptr[i] read past the end of the weights buffer, leaking adjacent heap floats into current_total_weight/current_squares_weight and the output (CWE-125), or crashing on an unmapped page.
findings.what_to_confirm: Check the Prepare function for an assertion that weights has num_lookups elements; if absent, the read is unbounded.

findings.title: indices matrix indexed by num_lookups rows without validating its row count
findings.invariant: SizeOfDimension(indices, 0) must equal num_lookups so that indices->data.i32[i*lookup_rank + k] stays within the indices buffer for all i in [0,num_lookups), k in [0,lookup_rank-1).
findings.failure_mode: num_lookups comes from ids while lookup_rank comes from indices dimension 1; the code never checks that indices has num_lookups rows. A model whose indices tensor has fewer rows than ids has entries causes example_indices_offset + k to walk past the indices buffer (CWE-125 over-read), and the leaked values further steer the unbounded output_bucket/output_offset.
findings.what_to_confirm: Verify Prepare enforces indices.dims[0] == num_lookups; without it the inner indices read is unbounded.

findings.title: Signed integer overflow / size_t-to-int truncation in bucket-offset arithmetic
findings.anchor: output_bucket += ... * stride; stride *= dense_shape->data.i32[k]; const int output_offset = output_bucket * embedding_size;
findings.invariant: Bucket/offset arithmetic must be performed in a width that cannot overflow and every offset must be range-checked before it is used as a pointer base; multiplying an int output_bucket by size_t embedding_size and truncating back to int must not silently wrap.
findings.failure_mode: stride *= dense_shape->data.i32[k] and output_bucket += coord*stride are signed-int operations on attacker data and can overflow (UB); output_offset = output_bucket * embedding_size mixes int and size_t then truncates into an int. The resulting wild output_offset flows into current_output_offset. The per-element scatter loop re-checks it, but FinalizeAggregation (finding #2) does not — so this arithmetic is the source of the out-of-bounds offset consumed there.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm the offset math width and whether any clamp is applied to output_offset/current_output_offset before non-loop uses.

findings.title: Unchecked reinterpret_cast of node->builtin_data
findings.bug_class: Incorrect type conversion / possible NULL dereference of loader-supplied pointer
findings.invariant: builtin_data must be non-null and actually point at a TfLiteEmbeddingLookupSparseParams before params->combiner is dereferenced (lines 94, 123).
findings.failure_mode: reinterpret_cast performs no runtime check. If a malformed model leaves builtin_data null or of a mismatched type, dereferencing params->combiner is a type-confused read or NULL dereference (CWE-704/CWE-476). Normally the flatbuffer parser guarantees the type, so this is a latent, low-severity contract on loader trust.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm the interpreter always populates builtin_data for this op and that params is non-null before use.

audit_checklist: Confirm lookup_size * embedding_size (line 54) and output_size * sizeof(float) (line 55) are overflow-checked before TfLiteTensorRealloc, and that ResizeTensor's shape cannot exceed the reallocated buffer.

Inspect FinalizeAggregation: does it write embedding_size floats to the passed pointer, and is current_output_offset (lines 96,125) bounds-checked to [0, output_size - embedding_size] before the call?
Verify Prepare (not shown) enforces NumElements(weights) >= num_lookups so weights_ptr[i] (line 108) cannot over-read.
Verify Prepare enforces SizeOfDimension(indices, 0) == num_lookups so indices->data.i32[i*lookup_rank+k] (line 86) stays in bounds.
Check that dense_shape entries used as dims (line 38) and strides (line 87) are validated non-negative, since negatives widen to huge size_t and cause signed int overflow in stride/output_bucket.
Confirm node->builtin_data is guaranteed non-null and correctly typed before params->combiner is dereferenced (lines 94,123).
Confirm TfLiteTensorRealloc failure is fully handled — the only signal used is output_ptr != nullptr (line 61); ensure weights_ptr/value_ptr are also valid.
